kiku.title: Community Feed
kiku.post_placeholder: Share your experience or ask a question...

trouble.title: Emergency Help
trouble.emergency_subtitle: Stay calm. Call 110 for Police, 119 for Ambulance or Fire.

phone.title: Phone Calls & Noise on Transit
phone.desc: Talking on the phone is considered rude on trains and buses in Japan. Set your phone to silent mode. Be aware of sound leaking from earphones. Near priority seats, it's polite to turn your phone off entirely.

backpack.title: Backpacks & Large Bags
backpack.desc: On crowded trains, carry your backpack in front of you or place it on the overhead rack. Keep large suitcases out of doorways so others can board and exit easily.
backpack.good: Carry in front or use overhead rack

queue.title: Lining Up to Board
queue.desc: Platforms in Japan have marked boarding positions. Line up in order and wait for everyone to exit the train before boarding. Cutting in line is considered extremely rude.
queue.good: Wait in line for your turn

priority.title: Priority Seats
priority.desc: Priority seats are reserved for elderly passengers, pregnant women, people with disabilities, and those with small children. If you see someone who needs the seat, please offer yours.
priority.good: Offer your seat to those in need
priority.bad: Pretend to sleep in priority seats

eating.title: Eating on Trains
eating.desc: Eating on commuter trains and buses is generally not acceptable. However, eating is fine on bullet trains (Shinkansen) and limited express trains. Avoid strongly scented foods.

noise.title: Quiet Spaces
noise.desc: Libraries, hospitals, and museums are quiet zones. If you receive a phone call, step outside before answering. Keep your voice low in these spaces.

queue.title: Queuing Culture
queue.desc: Japanese people take queuing very seriously. Whether at ATMs, cash registers, or restrooms, always join the end of the line and wait your turn patiently.
queue.good: Join the end of the line

shoes.title: Removing Shoes
shoes.desc: You must remove your shoes at temples, traditional inns (ryokan), some restaurants, and clinics. A raised floor at the entrance is the signal to take shoes off. Use the provided slippers if available.
shoes.good: Remove shoes at the entrance step

trash.title: Few Public Trash Cans
trash.desc: Japan has very few public trash cans. Carry a small bag with you for your waste and dispose of it at your accommodation or at a convenience store. Littering is prohibited.

wash.title: Wash Before Entering the Bath
wash.desc: Before entering the communal bath, you must rinse your body or wash at the shower station. This is the most important bathing rule in Japan — it keeps the shared water clean for everyone.
wash.bad: Get into the bath without washing

towel.title: Towel Etiquette
towel.desc: Never put your towel in the bath water. Place your small towel on top of your head or on the edge of the bath. Leave your large towel in the changing room.
towel.good: Place towel on your head or shelf
towel.bad: Dip your towel in the bath

dry.title: Before Returning to the Changing Room
dry.desc: Before going back to the changing room, lightly towel off your body while still in the bathing area. Walking back dripping wet makes the floor slippery and is inconsiderate to others.
dry.good: Towel off in the bathing area

tattoo.title: Tattoos
tattoo.desc: Many onsen and sento (public baths) prohibit entry for people with tattoos. Check in advance if tattoos are accepted, or consider using private baths (kashikiri buro).
tattoo.bad: Enter a no-tattoo facility without asking

swim.title: Swimwear
swim.desc: Swimwear is not allowed in Japanese onsen and sento (except for some mixed-gender water park facilities). Bathing nude is the standard practice.

itadakimasu.title: "Itadakimasu" & "Gochisousama"
itadakimasu.desc: Say "itadakimasu" (I humbly receive) before eating, and "gochisousama deshita" (thank you for the meal) after. This beautiful Japanese custom expresses gratitude for the food and the people who prepared it.
itadakimasu.good: Say greetings before and after meals

chopsticks.title: Chopstick Etiquette
chopsticks.desc: Never stick chopsticks upright in rice — this resembles a funeral ritual and is deeply offensive. Also never pass food chopstick-to-chopstick, as this mirrors another funeral custom. Avoid hovering chopsticks over dishes indecisively.

slurp.title: Slurping Noodles
slurp.desc: In Japan, it's perfectly acceptable to slurp noodles like ramen and soba. In fact, it's considered a way to show you're enjoying the food and to enhance the flavor.
slurp.bad: Worry too much about making noise

tipping.title: No Tipping Culture
tipping.desc: Japan has no tipping culture. Tips are not expected at restaurants, taxis, or hotels. Leaving a tip may actually cause confusion or embarrassment. Good service is simply the standard.
tipping.bad: Leave a tip on the table

oshibori.title: Oshibori (Hot Towel) Usage
oshibori.desc: Restaurants in Japan often provide an oshibori (warm towel). It is meant for wiping your hands only. Using it to wipe your face or neck is considered poor manners.
oshibori.good: Use it to wipe your hands

cash.title: Cash Tray Payment
cash.desc: At registers, place your money on the cash tray rather than handing it directly to the cashier. This is the Japanese way. Cashless payment (IC cards, QR codes) is also widely accepted.
cash.bad: Hand money directly to the cashier

bag.title: Plastic Bags Are Charged
bag.desc: Since 2020, plastic bags are charged at all stores in Japan. Bring your own eco-bag (reusable bag). At convenience stores, you'll be asked "Do you need a bag?"

taxfree.title: Tax-Free Shopping
taxfree.desc: Foreign tourists can get a consumption tax exemption at qualifying stores when spending ¥5,000 or more. You'll need your passport. Tax-free items must not be opened until you leave Japan.

fitting.title: Fitting Room Etiquette
fitting.desc: When trying on clothes, always ask a staff member before using the fitting room. Use the face cover provided to prevent makeup from staining the garments.
fitting.bad: Enter the fitting room without asking

shoes.title: Indoor Footwear
shoes.desc: At ryokan (traditional inns) and some hotel areas, remove your shoes at the entrance and switch to slippers. On tatami mat floors, remove even the slippers — walk in socks or barefoot.

yukata.title: How to Wear a Yukata
yukata.desc: Ryokan provide yukata (light cotton robes). Wrap the left side over the right (as seen from outside). Wrapping right over left is reserved for dressing the deceased — an important detail to remember.

checkout.title: Check-out Time
checkout.desc: Japanese accommodations are strict about check-out times, usually 10:00–11:00 AM. If you need a late check-out, always ask the front desk in advance.

quiet.title: Quiet Hours
quiet.desc: At ryokan and hotels, keep quiet after 10 PM. Ryokan in particular may have thin walls, so being considerate of neighboring rooms is important.

shrine.title: Visiting Shrines & Temples
shrine.desc: At Shinto shrines, follow the ritual: bow twice, clap twice, bow once. Bow before passing through the torii gate and walk along the sides of the path (the center is reserved for gods). At Buddhist temples, press your palms together without clapping.
shrine.good: Two bows, two claps, one bow (shrines)
shrine.bad: Walk down the center of the path

bowing.title: Bowing Culture
bowing.desc: In Japan, bowing replaces handshaking. A 15° bow is for greetings, 30° for thank you, and 45° for deep apology. Simply follow the other person's lead and bow naturally in return.

gifts.title: Gift-Giving Etiquette
gifts.desc: Give and receive gifts with both hands. It's polite to be modest when opening gifts in front of others. Avoid sets of 4 items, as the number four sounds like "death" (shi) in Japanese.
gifts.good: Give and receive with both hands
gifts.bad: Hand over carelessly with one hand

photo.title: Photography Manners
photo.desc: Avoid photographing people without permission. Many temples, shrines, and museums have restricted photography areas. Always check for signs before taking photos.
photo.good: Check if photography is allowed first

sorting.title: Waste Sorting
sorting.desc: Japan requires detailed waste sorting: burnable, non-burnable, recyclable, PET bottles, cans, and glass. Sorting rules vary by municipality, so check the local guidelines carefully.

carry.title: Carry Your Trash
carry.desc: There are very few trash cans on Japanese streets. Carry a small bag with you for your waste. This practice is the foundation of Japan's famously clean streets.
carry.good: Carry a trash bag with you

convenience.title: Convenience Store Bins
convenience.desc: Trash cans outside convenience stores are intended only for items purchased at that store. Bringing household waste or trash from other stores is prohibited.
convenience.good: Only dispose of items bought there

recycle.title: Recycling Awareness
recycle.desc: Separate PET bottles into cap, label, and bottle before disposal. Recycling bins next to vending machines are for cans and PET bottles only.

chat.title: Mana Assistant
chat.subtitle: AI Concierge
chat.welcome_desc: Ask me anything about the RUUMANA app, Japanese culture, or daily life in Japan.
chat.suggestion_1: How do I use this app?

events.jan1: New Year's Day — The beginning of a new year
events.jan7: Nanakusa no Sekku — A day to eat seven-herb rice porridge
events.feb3: Setsubun — Bean-throwing to drive away evil spirits
events.mar3: Hinamatsuri (Girls' Day) — Wishing for girls' healthy growth
events.may5: Children's Day — Tango no Sekku festival
events.jul7: Tanabata — Write your wishes on paper strips
events.aug15: Memorial Day for End of WWII
events.dec31: New Year's Eve — Eat toshikoshi soba (year-crossing noodles)

trouble_steps.medical_1: Stay calm and check your surroundings for safety.
trouble_steps.medical_2: If unconscious or bleeding heavily, call 119 immediately.
trouble_steps.medical_3: Find the nearest AED if necessary.
trouble_steps.medical_4: Prepare your passport and insurance information.
trouble_steps.lost_1: Go to the nearest Koban (Police Box).
trouble_steps.lost_2: Fill out a Lost Property Report (Ishitsubutsu-todoke).
trouble_steps.lost_3: If you lost a credit card, contact your bank immediately.
trouble_steps.lost_4: Check the station office if lost on a train.
trouble_steps.disaster_1: Drop, Cover, and Hold On during an earthquake.
trouble_steps.disaster_2: Check the Mirumana map for the nearest evacuation shelter.
trouble_steps.disaster_3: Follow the instructions of local staff or police.
trouble_steps.disaster_4: Use Disaster Message Board (171) to contact family.

animation.subtitle: ru-mana intro animation prototypes
animation.brush_torii_desc: SVG animation weaving ink brushstrokes and a torii gate
animation.flags_gathering_title: Flags of the World, United in a Circle
animation.flags_gathering_desc: 36 national flags drift across the screen and gather into one circle
animation.circle_of_hands_title: Holding Hands Made the World Round
animation.circle_of_hands_desc: People from around the world walk together and join hands in a circle
animation.walking_together_desc: Each person walks at their own pace, forming one circle together
animation.flags_particles_desc: National flags become particles that paint the screen
animation.just_one_human_desc: Before any label, there is one human — a philosophical animation
animation.human_first_desc: Glassmorphism animation with a Human First theme

intro.gallery_hint: You can view animations anytime from the Gallery on the top page

landing.concept_body: We help you learn about cultural differences and manners in a fun way—not just language. "No judgment, no conflict"—let cultural differences become opportunities for connection.
landing.msg_door: Culture is a door, not a wall.
landing.feature_kiku: Share and listen in the community
landing.cta_subtitle: Use it in your browser right away, or install it as an app.
landing.install_native_desc: Add to your home screen for quick, app-like access.
landing.install_manual_desc: Follow the steps for your browser to install.
landing.install_ios_step1: Tap the Share button (□↑) at the bottom
landing.install_android_step2: Select "Add to Home Screen" or "Install App"
landing.install_pc_step1: Click the install icon in the address bar
